LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT GRANT KWOK'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND IS GRANTED AS TO THE MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT AND TO QUASH SUBSTITUTED SERVICE OF SUMMONS. ON JULY 1, 2013, PLAINTIFF WITHDREW HIS OPPOSITION TO MR. KWOK'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT AND TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS. PLAINTIFF NOW STATES THAT MR. KWOK HAS BEEN PERSONALLY SERVED WITH A SUMMONS AND COMPLAINT. THE COURT FINDS THAT THE FILING OF THE SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT ALLEGES ADDITIONAL DIFFERENT FACTS THAN THE COMPLAINT, AND THE SERVICE OF SUMMONS RUNS FROM THE DATE OF AMENDMENT. (BARRINGTON V. A. H. ROBINS CO. (1985) 39 CAL.3D 146, 154.) NOW THAT THE DEFAULT HAS BEEN SET ASIDE, DEFENDANT KWOK MAY RESPOND TO THE SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT IN COMPLIANCE WITH THE TIMING REQUIREMENTS IN THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: MARLA J. MILLER, CLERK: LESLEY FISCELLA, REPORTER: ARLENE HOWELL CSR2542.

LAW AND MOTION, 302, DEFENDANT MILLIE LUONG'S MOTION FOR MANDATORY DISMISSAL OF ASCARRUNZ'S ACTION FOR FAILURE TO BRING TO TRIAL IN FIVE YEARS IS DENIED. THE ORDER DATED JUNE 30, 2014, DOES NOT APPLY TO THE CROSS-COMPLAINT. THE UNDERLYING MOTION WAS ONLY DIRECTED TO PLAINTIFF'S COMPLAINT. CCP 583.310 DOES NOT MANDATE DISMISSAL OF THE CROSS-COMPLAINT. THE 5-YEAR PERIOD IS MEASURED FROM THE DATE OF FILING OF THAT PLEADING. SEE PEREZ V. GRAJALES (2008) 169 CAL.APP.4TH 580, 590. MS. LUONG'S ARGUMENT THAT THE COMPLAINT AND CROSS-COMPLAINT ARE THE SAME AND/OR THERE IS AN IMPROPER SPLITTING OF CLAIMS NEEDS TO BE RAISED IN A DIFFERENT MOTION. LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT HOWARD HSU'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT AND QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS. GRANTED AS TO THE MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT, AND DENIED AS TO THE MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS. DEFAULT IS SET ASIDE BECAUSE PLAINTIFF FAILED TO COMPLY WITH THE REQUIREMENT OF CCP 587 WHEN HE MAILED THE REQUEST FOR ENTRY OF DEFAULT TO A INCORRECT ADDRESS FOR DEFENDANT HSU. (SEE HSU DECLARATION IN SUPPORT OF MOTION, EX. B.) PLAINTIFF CONCEDES THAT BOTH THE NAME OF THE STREET AND ZIP CODE ARE INCORRECT. ALTHOUGH TITLED A MOTION TO QUASH, DEFENDANT HSU IN ESSENCE MOVES TO DISMISS PURSUANT TO CCP 583.250. THE COURT FINDS THAT THE FILING OF THE SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT ALLEGES ADDITIONAL DIFFERENT FACTS THAN THE COMPLAINT, AND THE SERVICE OF SUMMONS RUNS FROM THE DATE OF AMENDMENT. (BARRINGTON V. A. H. ROBINS CO. (1985) 39 CAL.3D 146, 154.) NOW THAT THE DEFAULT HAS BEEN SET ASIDE, AND DEFENDANT HSU ADMITS HE HAS BEEN PROPERLY SERVED, DEFENDANT HSU MAY RESPOND TO THE SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT IN COMPLIANCE WITH THE TIMING REQUIREMENTS IN THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: MARLA J. MILLER, CLERK: LESLEY FISCELLA, REPORTER: MELANIE GHENO CSR#7489.

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT MILLIE LUONG'S PARTIAL MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS IS GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. AS TO THE 6TH CAUSE OF ACTION (BREACH OF CONTRACT), THE MOTION IS DENIED. THE SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT ALLEGES THAT DEFENDANTS AGREED TO PAY PLAINTIFF'S ATTORNEYS FEES AND COSTS SHOULD HE COMMENCE LITIGATION AFTER TWO YEARS IN EXCHANGE FOR PLAINTIFF NOT FILING SUIT FOR TWO YEARS. PROMISING TO PAY ATTORNEYS' FEES AND COSTS IS CONSIDERATION FOR THE AGREEMENT. (SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT 49(F).) AS TO THE 7TH CAUSE OF ACTION (PROMISE WITHOUT INTENT TO PERFORM), THE MOTION IS GRANTED WITH 10 DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND IF PLAINTIFF CAN PLEAD FACTS, IN GOOD FAITH, TO ESTABLISH HE SUSTAINED DAMAGE BY WAITING TWO YEARS BEFORE FILING SUIT. AS TO THE 9TH CAUSE OF ACTION (DECLARATORY RELIEF), THE MOTION IS GRANTED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. THIS CAUSE OF ACTION IS REDUNDANT, AS PLAINTIFF'S BREACH OF CONTRACT CLAIMS HAVE ALREADY MATURED. (SEE JOLLEY V. CHASE HOME FINANCE LLC (2013) 213 CAL.APP.4TH 872, 909-10, HOLDING WHERE THERE IS "A FULLY MATURED CAUSE OF ACTION FOR MONEY, [PLAINTIFF] MUST SEEK DAMAGES, AND NOT PURSUE DECLARATORY RELIEF.") THE COURT GRANTED 20 DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: MARLA J. MILLER, CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; COURT REPORTER: ANTHONY C. VAUGHN, CSR # 6185

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorney Fees And Costs Date: August 18, 2014 Time: 9:30 A.M. Location: Dept. 302 (Reservation #070814-04) Civil Codeâ§ 1717
Off Calendar Sep-26-2014 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. KENNETH E. MCDONALD OF LERNER & MCDONALD, TEL. (714)850-9000 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF BY TELEPHONE APPEARANCE;
ATTY. THOMAS F. OGDEN, TEL. NO. (626)344-8270 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT MILLIE LUONG BY TELEPHONE APPEARANCE.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. CROSS-DEFENDANT MILLIE LUONG's Motion For Attorney Fees And Costs- The motion for attorney's fees is granted. In Reynolds Metals Co. v. Alperson (1979) 25 Cal.3d 124, the plaintiff brought suit to collect a note signed by the corporation, suing individual defendants on an alter ego theory. The Reynolds court recognized that the individual defendants, though nonsignatories to the note, would have been liable for attorney fees had plaintiff prevailed in establishing alter ego liability. Accordingly, the demands of reciprocity under Civil Code section 1717 required that such section be "interpreted to further provide a reciprocal remedy for a nonsignatory defendant, sued on a contract as if he were a party to it, when a plaintiff would clearly be entitled to attorney's fees should he prevail in enforcing the contractual obligation against the defendant." Reynolds, 25 Cal.3d at 128. In this case, paragraph 39 of the 3rd Amended Complaint alleges that all of the defendants are alter egos of each other and Ms. Luong was named as a defendant on the breach of contract (Asset Purchase Agreement) cause of action (3rd Amended Complaint, 26:13). Ms. Luong prevailed on the Third Amended Complaint when she obtained a dismissal of that pleading. Therefore, she is entitled to fees per Reynolds. If Mr. Ascarrunz prevailed on his breach of contract and alter ego claims, he would have been entitled to seek fees from Ms. Luong.
Mr. Ascarrunz's cases are distinguishable. In Leach v. Home Savings & Loan Assn. (1986) 185 Cal.App.3d 1295, a signatory defendant sought fees against a nonsignatory plaintiff and the nonsignatory plaintiff would have been unable to recover fees if he prevailed. In Sweat Sweat v. Hollister (1995) 37 Cal.App.4th 603, 616, the party claiming fees was not a party to the germane contract. The court explained that a party must base the fee claim on a statute or contract and the doctrine of equitable estoppel does not allow the recovery of fees. In Bear Creek Planning Committee v. Ferwerda (2011) 193 Cal.App.4th 1178, the court found that there was no binding fee clause and the estoppel theory failed.
The court recognizes that Mr. Ascarrunz's cross-complaint is still pending and the court asked the parties to brief whether that pleading affects this motion. The parties chose not to brief the issue and the court finds that the motion can proceed. The cross-complaint is not an action "on a contract" within the meaning of CC 1717 and the only pleading that implicated CC 1717 was the 3rd Amended Complaint.
Ms. Luong is entitled to recover a total of $125,000 as reasonable attorney's fees for prevailing on the 3rd Amended Complaint. The fees claimed in her motion are excessive; the billing statements are padded and unclear. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sheryl L. Sawyer, CSR # 5976

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-11-2013 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: WILLIAM HUGHES, ESQ., 415.397.2020, FOR PLAINTIFF CASAR ASCARRUNZ.
CAROL JASINSKI, ESQ,. 415.393.9566, FOR DEFENDANT GRANT KWOK.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling: DEFENDANT GRANT KWOK's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT is sustained with 10 days (from notice) leave to amend as to all causes of action. Defendant's Request for Judicial Notice is granted. Per the August 2, 2013 Order of this Court, the Second Amended Complaint does not relate back to the original complaint because additional and different facts were alleged. The SAC was filed on July 28, 2011, after the Statute of Limitations had run for all causes of action. (See CCP 337 - 338.) Plaintiff's position that the demurrer should be overruled because it was calendared 38 days after the filing of the demurrer is rejected. CRC 3.1320(d) states that "demurrers must be set for hearing not more than 35 days following the filing of the demurrer or on the first date available to the court thereafter." Plaintiff has produced no evidence that the demurrer was not scheduled on the first date available to the court. Additionally, even if the hearing was set 38 days out in error, this error is de minimus. Plaintiff does not argue that this error materially prejudiced him in any way. Further, it is a general legal principle that deciding cases on substantive issues rather than procedural grounds is preferred, especially when the procedural issue is de minimus. Order signed in open court. Judge: Leslie C. Nichols; Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR #6185

Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Partial Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Oct-03-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. WILLIAM HUGHES, TEL. NO. 397-2020 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. THOMAS OGDEN APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT BY TELEPHONE APPEARANCE.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant Millie Luong's Partial Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings is granted in part and denied in part. As to the 6th cause of action (Breach of Contract), the motion is denied. The Second Amended Complaint alleges that Defendants agreed to pay Plaintiff's attorneys fees and costs should he commence litigation after two years in exchange for Plaintiff not filing suit for two years. Promising to pay attorneys' fees and costs is consideration for the agreement. (Second Amended Complaint 49(f).) As to the 7th cause of action (Promise without Intent to Perform), the motion is granted with 10 days leave to amend if Plaintiff can plead facts, in good faith, to establish he sustained damage by waiting two years before filing suit. As to the 9th cause of action (Declaratory Relief), the motion is granted without leave to amend. This cause of action is redundant, as Plaintiff's breach of contract claims have already matured. (See Jolley v. Chase Home Finance LLC (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 872, 909-10, holding where there is "a fully matured cause of action for money, [plaintiff] must seek damages, and not pursue declaratory relief.") The court granted 20 days leave to amend. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Marla J. Miller, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Court Reporter: Anthony C. Vaughn, CSR # 6185

MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
Off Calendar Jul-31-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearance on this matter. RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling re: DEFENDANT HOWARD HSU'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT and quash service of summons. Granted as to the motion to set aside default, and denied as to the motion to quash service of summons. Default is set aside because Plaintiff failed to comply with the requirement of CCP 587 when he mailed the request for entry of default to a incorrect address for Defendant Hsu. (See Hsu Declaration in support of motion, Ex. B.) Plaintiff concedes that both the name of the street and zip code are incorrect. Although titled a motion to quash, Defendant Hsu in essence moves to dismiss pursuant to CCP 583.250. The Court finds that the filing of the Second Amended Complaint alleges additional different facts than the Complaint, and the service of summons runs from the date of amendment. (Barrington v. A. H. Robins Co. (1985) 39 Cal.3d 146, 154.) Now that the default has been set aside, and Defendant Hsu admits he has been properly served, Defendant Hsu may respond to the Second Amended Complaint in compliance with the timing requirements in the Code of Civil Procedure. Order signed in open court. Judge: Marla J. Miller, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Melanie Gheno CSR#7489.
